metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS3: Verify that the `mcp` Python SDK at the declared `>=1.2` floor exposes `ToolAnnotations` (or equivalent) for annotating read-only/destructive/idempotent/open-world hints, and update the version floor in `pyproject.toml` if a higher minimum is required.
findings.finding: WS3 asserts MCP clients can render behavior hints (read-only / destructive / idempotent / open-world) from tool annotations, but the plan does not verify that the pinned `mcp>=1.2` SDK version actually exposes ToolAnnotations — this is an unverified external-integration capability assertion.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 / Scope IN item 3 ('Annotate MCP tools with behavior hints')
findings.suggested_fix: Check the modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk changelog for the release that added ToolAnnotations support; if it is >1.2, update `pyproject.toml` `mcp` extra floor to match and document the constraint in the WS3 child story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS10: Specify whether TypedDicts are hand-authored (following the `_snapshot_schema.py` precedent) or auto-generated (naming the tool, e.g. `datamodel-code-generator`), and name the CI drift-gate mechanism (e.g. regenerate-and-diff script akin to the prompt-index gate, or extension of the existing schema golden-hash tests).
findings.finding: WS10 introduces 'schema-derived TypedDicts with a CI drift-gate' as a novel pattern without referencing the existing hand-authored TypedDict-from-schema precedent in the codebase (`_snapshot_schema.py`) or evaluating established prior art (datamodel-code-generator, Pydantic's model_json_schema import) for the generation approach, leaving the implementation strategy and drift-gate mechanism unspecified.
findings.location: Scope IN item 10 / Success Criteria bullet 9 ('schema-derived TypedDicts, drift-gated')
findings.suggested_fix: Reference `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_snapshot_schema.py` as the established precedent for hand-authored TypedDicts and extend the existing `tests/interfaces/contracts/` golden-hash pattern for drift detection, explicitly calling out this approach in the WS10 child story to avoid reinvention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS3 must define the exact MCP spec version and field names (`readOnlyHint`/`destructiveHint`/`idempotentHint`/`openWorldHint` or equivalent) and provide a per-tool assignment table mapping each of the ~45 `@mcp.tool()` registrations to its correct hint values.
findings.finding: WS3 references a four-term behavior-hint vocabulary ('read-only / destructive / idempotent / open-world') that must be annotated onto ~45 existing MCP tools, but the plan never defines the mapping from these human-readable terms to the actual MCP spec annotation fields (e.g., the MCP 2025-03 spec uses boolean fields `readOnlyHint`, `destructiveHint`, `idempotentHint`, `openWorldHint`). A child-story implementer must independently resolve which spec version, which field names, and which value applies to each of the ~45 `@mcp.tool()` registrations.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'MCP clients can render safe UI from tool behavior hints (read-only / destructive / idempotent / open-world)' / Scope WS3: 'Annotate MCP tools with behavior hints.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS3 scope: (1) pin the MCP annotation spec version (e.g., MCP 2025-03 Tool.annotations); (2) define a table in the child ticket listing each tool name, its classification (read-only vs. write, idempotent vs. not, open-world vs. closed), and the resulting boolean hint values; (3) require a test asserting each registered tool's FastMCP metadata carries the expected hint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS2 must specify the drift-guard's decision rule: which authoritative source it reads (e.g., the env-var table in `config.py`), what fields in `server.json` it validates against it, where the check script lives, and what CI failure mode it uses.
findings.finding: WS2 ('Fix server.json env/config contract + add a CI drift-guard') references a CI drift-guard but never defines the decision rule for what constitutes drift — specifically: what the CI job compares (env var names only? descriptions? required flags? the full JSON structure?), where the guard script lives, or what the failure mode is when drift is detected.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The MCP `server.json` manifest matches the real config/env contract and is protected from silent re-drift by a CI gate.' / Scope WS2.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` in `release.yml` currently generates PEP 740 attestations (check for `attestations: true`); if not, update WS5 scope to ADD attestation generation (`attestations: true` in the publish step) rather than merely documenting an already-emitted feature.
findings.finding: WS5 asserts 'the PyPI attestations the release already emits' should be documented, but `release.yml` uses `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` with no `attestations: true` flag and no `actions/attest-build-provenance` step — meaning PEP 740 attestations are NOT currently generated. The plan's doc-only premise is therefore based on an unverified (and likely false) capability claim.
findings.location: Scope · IN item 5 ('Supply-chain CI hardening … attestation docs'); Success Criteria item 5 ('documents the PyPI attestations the release already emits')
findings.suggested_fix: In release.yml, add `attestations: true` to the `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` step (requires `id-token: write` permission, already present on the `publish` job). Then WS5 can truthfully document attestations. Update the plan's premise from 'already emits' to 'will emit after WS5 lands'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ordering note to b593: 'Should land after or together with 3ee0 (community health files), which creates the SECURITY.md the Security URL references.'
findings.finding: b593 (pyproject metadata) references a Security URL pointing to SECURITY.md, which is delivered by 3ee0 (community health files), but no ordering dependency between the two children is stated.
findings.suggested_fix: In b593's scope or AC, add: 'Blocked by / land after 3ee0; alternatively, include SECURITY.md creation in the same change if b593 lands first.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile SC7 and Scope OUT by either (a) changing 'representative measured numbers' to 'representative estimated/anecdotal numbers' in SC7, or (b) adding a one-time manual benchmark run as an explicit in-scope task for WS7 while keeping the CI harness deferred.
findings.finding: Success Criteria SC7 requires 'representative measured numbers' for the scale envelope, but Scope OUT explicitly defers the 'CI benchmark harness' and WS7 is described as a 'docs-only scale envelope.' These cross-section claims contradict each other: 'measured numbers' implies empirical data collection, while 'docs-only' with no harness leaves it unclear whether numbers will be hand-gathered once, fabricated, or simply omitted — sending the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Success Criteria (SC7) vs Scope OUT ('CI benchmark harness — deferred; docs-only scale envelope (WS7) instead')
findings.suggested_fix: In SC7, replace 'representative measured numbers' with 'representative numbers (may be manually gathered; CI benchmark harness is deferred)' to align with the 'docs-only' WS7 framing in Scope OUT.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS2: Explicitly state whether `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` is retained (with a `deprecated: true` annotation) or removed from `server.json`; if removed, add an expand-contract note (keep it one release, then drop) and a rollback path in the story.
findings.finding: The plan fixes server.json by adding the two omitted env vars but does not specify the fate of the already-advertised deprecated `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` entry — if it is removed from the manifest, that is a breaking change to a public contract artifact with no expand-contract sequence or rollback path stated.
findings.location: Scope IN · WS2 'Fix server.json env/config contract + add a CI drift-guard' / Success Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Retain the entry in server.json for at least one release cycle with a `deprecated: true` / `removal_version` field, document the migration in the changelog, then remove it in a subsequent story. State this sequence explicitly in the WS2 child story AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note to the epic identifying which workstreams (suggested: WS5 and WS10 as highest-uncertainty CI/tooling changes) should land as early de-risking probes, and which lower-risk doc workstreams (WS1, WS4, WS6, WS7, WS8, WS9) can follow in parallel.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a sequencing or ordering of the 10 workstreams, leaving open a horizontal big-bang delivery risk: all 10 child tickets could be opened and worked in parallel without a de-risking MVP slice identified first. The highest-uncertainty items (WS5: CodeQL/pip-audit CI integration; WS10: schema-derived TypedDicts with drift-gate) could surface unexpected blockers late if not sequenced early.
findings.location: Scope / IN (10 workstreams) and Acceptance Criteria sections — no ordering stated

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering constraint: 830d (stability matrix) must land after 3a10 (TypedDicts) so the matrix accurately reflects the delivered typed contract.
findings.finding: 830d (API-stability matrix) depends on 3a10 (TypedDicts) for an accurate Python-facade row, but no ordering constraint is stated — if 830d lands first, it will document a typed contract that does not yet exist.
findings.suggested_fix: In 830d's acceptance criteria or scope, add: 'Depends on 3a10 (TypedDicts story) having landed; the Python-facade row of the matrix should cross-reference the generated TypedDicts rather than describing them inline.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In WS2, explicitly acknowledge that removing `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` from server.json is a breaking manifest change and add a migration note (e.g., deprecation comment in the manifest or a CHANGELOG entry) for existing MCP client authors.
findings.finding: WS2 (Fix server.json) plans to remove the deprecated `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` entry and add the two omitted env vars, but the plan does not acknowledge existing MCP client configurations that may reference the old manifest shape. Removing an advertised key is a breaking change for any client that reads server.json to build its config UI or validation.
findings.location: Scope · IN item 2 / Success Criteria SC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `deprecated_env_vars` or `notes` field in server.json listing `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` as removed-in-this-release, and include a one-line CHANGELOG entry so integrators are notified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Coordinate landing order for fce6 and e110 (or implement them in the same change): e110 (docstring fix) should land first as it edits the run_workflow function body, then fce6 adds the ToolAnnotations decorator.
findings.finding: fce6 (MCP annotations) and e110 (run_workflow docstring fix) both modify `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` with no stated coordination, creating a merge conflict risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to both children: 'Depends on / should be sequenced with the sibling story that also touches _mcp_writes.py.' Prefer landing e110 before fce6 so fce6's annotation is applied to the corrected docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (suggest 3bf6, which already owns the README Quickstart restructure) as the integration point for all README link additions, and have 830d and d063 depend on or coordinate with it.
findings.finding: Multiple children (3bf6, 830d, d063, and implicitly b593 via the Security URL) all modify or add links to README.md with no stated integration order, creating an implicit shared-state / merge-conflict risk on that file.
findings.location: child 3bf6-de76-bdd4-4096, child 830d-1662-c8be-45f4, child d063-90d1-1c60-4f0a, child b593-86d1-b28d-4646
findings.suggested_fix: In 830d and d063, replace 'link from README' with a note that the README link will be added as part of 3bf6's README pass or in a final integration step, avoiding parallel README edits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit task in WS2 and WS10 to grep/audit existing docs, READMEs, and per-function docstrings for references to the old env-var list and 'bare dict' return descriptions, and update or replace any stale text before the respective stories close.
findings.finding: The plan changes server.json content and introduces TypedDicts as the documented return contract, but does not call for auditing existing docs/docstrings/READMEs that may contain stale references to the old three-env-var manifest or to 'returns a bare dict' — those references will become incorrect after the workstreams land.
findings.location: Scope IN · WS2 and WS10; Acceptance Criteria generally
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS2 child story AC, add: 'All README/doc references to rebar env vars are updated to match the corrected server.json.' In the WS10 child story AC, add: 'Existing per-function docstrings that describe return type as plain dict are replaced with TypedDict references.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one owner for the canonical exception-surface documentation (suggest: 3a10 owns the full exception docs; 830d's stability matrix cross-links to it rather than re-stating it).
findings.finding: 830d (API-stability matrix) and 3a10 (TypedDicts) both independently document the rebar.* Python facade exception surface (RebarError/ConcurrencyError), creating a scope overlap with no designated single source of truth.
findings.suggested_fix: In 830d's scope, replace inline exception documentation with a pointer: 'Exception surface: see TypedDicts story / rebar._errors module docs.' In 3a10, mark the exception docs as the canonical source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the schema-derived TypedDicts pattern: which schemas are sources of truth, what command regenerates the types, what the CI drift-gate checks, and why this approach was chosen over alternatives (hand-written TypedDicts, dataclasses, Pydantic).
findings.finding: WS10 introduces a novel project pattern — schema-derived TypedDicts with a CI drift-gate — that creates a new coupling between JSON schema files and the Python facade layer, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is planned to capture the decision rationale, the derivation mechanism, or the maintenance contract for future contributors.
findings.location: Scope > IN, workstream 10 ('Typed public library models (schema-derived TypedDicts, drift-gated)')
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-typed-facade-models.md` (or a `## Typed facade models` section in CLAUDE.md) covering: source schemas, generation command (e.g. `make gen-types`), drift-gate mechanism (e.g. `make gen-types && git diff --exit-code`), and the static-only constraint rationale. Reference this from the WS10 child story AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one dogfooding / end-to-end validation criterion — e.g., 'A contributor unfamiliar with rebar can execute every command in the quickstart against a local checkout without error' or a CI smoke-test that imports the typed facade and passes mypy.
findings.finding: No Success Criterion includes a concrete end-to-end validation mechanism (dogfooding, before/after workflow, or measurable target) — all criteria are 'artifact exists' or 'CI gate green' checks. This makes it impossible to verify that the improvements actually work for the named beneficiary (e.g., a newcomer can successfully onboard via the quickstart).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CI step in WS8 (quickstart) that runs each shell command in the quickstart via a script-doc test (e.g., `cram` or a simple `bash -e`), and add a mypy check in WS10 that imports the generated TypedDicts and validates a sample return value.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
